title: bind mounts not working without --privileged flag
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

When using podman on Fedora 31 as a replacement for docker, I've had issues getting bind mounts to continue working correctly.

Unless I pass the `--privileged` flag, I seem to also get a permission denied error when trying to access any of the files mounted inside the container.

All files are owned by the user that is running the container, and I've tried matching the user/group ids of the user inside the container to the outer host as well.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Clone https://github.com/mnahkies/podman-bind-mount-bug

2. Run `./outter-script.sh`

3. Observe that the `inner-script.sh` that was run within the container was not able to access the file that was mounted into the container.

**Describe the results you received:**
`cat: can't open '/opt/bug/data/my-data.txt': Permission denied`

**Describe the results you expected:**
`if you see this, then it worked!`

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**
```
podman version 1.7.0
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body: Are y ou running as root, or rootless

Running as my own user, which has the default uid/gid of 1000:1000

I've tried passing `-u 1000:1000` to the run command as well to no avail.
SELinux pops up as the most obvious cause to me. Does appending `:z` to the volume mount to relabel it (or running the container with `--security-opt label=disable`) resolve the issue?

Please show the command that does not work, and what errors you are seeing.
Most likely SELinux or User Namespace related.

The linked repository contains a minimal reproduction of the issue I was experiencing.

Appending `:z` does indeed work, thanks. Unfortunately my knowledge of SELinux is pretty limited. Why do the files need to be re-labelled? 

I see that on the docker documentation there is a warning that this could have unintended consequences due to the relabelling, and I notice that the relabelling removes the `unconfined_u` / `user_home_t` label and adds `system_u` / `container_file_t`
(ref: https://docs.docker.com/storage/bind-mounts/#configure-the-selinux-label)

Why did this work without relabelling using docker? Was it due to docker using root permissions?

Also tested using the `--security-opt label=disable` parameter, and can confirm that also worked (and did not modify the existing labels)

I'll close this, but if you don't mind explaining the reason why this becomes necessary when migrating to podman from docker I'd appreciate it - this has been my only real pain point so far and I'd like to better understand it
This should be identical between Docker and Podman - you may have been running Docker without SELinux support?
Definitely possible, I've been using fedora with docker since fedora 28 or so and don't believe I made any customisations to disable selinux, but I can spin up a VM and test that theory

Depending on the version of docker, native or upstream, it might not have had the --selinux-enabled flag passed into the daemon.  Without this flag SELinux separation for containers would be disabled.
